houseBrackmannGrade.description: Normal: normal facial function in all areas
Mild dysfunction: slight weakness, synkinesis barely noticeable; complete eye closure with minimal effort
Moderate dysfunction: obvious but not disfiguring asymmetry; synkinesis noticeable; complete eye closure with effort
Moderately severe: obvious weakness, disfiguring asymmetry; incomplete eye closure
Severe dysfunction: barely perceptible motion; incomplete eye closure, no forehead motion
Total paralysis: no movement, complete loss of tone

sunnybrookFacialGradingSystem: Composite score from 0 (complete paralysis) to 100 (normal function)
Assesses resting symmetry, voluntary movement, and synkinesis separately

historyFeatures.typicalPresentation: Acute onset unilateral lower motor neuron facial weakness developing over hours to 2-3 days
prodromalSymptoms: Post-auricular or retroauricular pain in 50-60% of patients (ipsilateral to palsy)
Facial numbness or paresthesias (not objective sensory loss)
Altered taste (dysgeusia) on anterior two-thirds of tongue due to chorda tympani involvement
Hyperacusis due to stapedius muscle paralysis (hearing seems too loud on affected side)
Reduced tearing or altered lacrimation ipsilaterally
Sense of facial stiffness or heaviness before visible weakness
historyFeatures.onset: Acute, maximal weakness within 72 hours; onset to maximal deficit usually 48 hours
associatedConditions: Recent upper respiratory infection or viral syndrome
Pregnancy (especially third trimester and immediate postpartum period)
naturalHistory: 70% recover completely, 85% have satisfactory recovery within 3-6 months
Recovery begins within 2-3 weeks in favorable cases
If no improvement by 3-4 weeks, prognosis is worse
Recurrence in approximately 10% of patients (ipsilateral or contralateral)

lowerMotorNeuronPattern: Weakness of entire ipsilateral face: both upper and lower face involved
Forehead: inability to wrinkle forehead, raise eyebrow (distinguishes from UMN stroke, which spares forehead)
Eye: incomplete eye closure, lagophthalmos, Bell phenomenon (eye rolls upward on attempted closure)
Mouth: drooping of corner of mouth, inability to smile or whistle, food pooling in buccal sulcus
Nasolabial fold effacement on affected side
cranialNerveExam: CN VII (facial): complete lower motor neuron pattern weakness
CN V (trigeminal): normal facial sensation (pain/numbness is subjective, not objective loss)
CN VIII (vestibulocochlear): normal hearing; check for hyperacusis (stapedius reflex absent)
Other cranial nerves must be normal - any additional cranial nerve abnormality suggests alternative diagnosis
specificBellPalsySigns: Bell phenomenon: upward deviation of eye on attempted closure (normal reflex, becomes visible with weakness)
Poor blink reflex on affected side
Reduced tearing on affected side (aberrant regeneration may cause tearing with eating - crocodile tears)
Taste disturbance on anterior two-thirds of tongue on affected side

riskFactors: Pregnancy: third trimester and first week postpartum (3-4 times increased risk)
Upper respiratory tract infection within preceding 2 weeks
Familial predisposition: 4-14% have positive family history
Recurrent Bell palsy risk increased in familial cases
Age: all ages affected but peak in 15-45 year range

subtypes.name: Classic Bell Palsy (Idiopathic)
subtypes.description: Acute peripheral facial nerve palsy without identifiable cause after appropriate evaluation
subtypes.details: Diagnosis of exclusion; accounts for 60-75% of all acute peripheral facial palsy cases; HSV-1 reactivation hypothesized but not proven causative

subtypes.name: Ramsay Hunt Syndrome (VZV)
subtypes.description: Facial palsy caused by varicella-zoster virus reactivation in geniculate ganglion
subtypes.details: Herpes zoster oticus: vesicular eruption on auricle, external auditory canal, tympanic membrane; severe pain out of proportion; worse prognosis than Bell palsy; requires antiviral therapy; accounts for 5-15% of facial palsies

subtypes.name: Lyme Disease Facial Palsy
subtypes.description: Facial nerve palsy due to Borrelia burgdorferi infection, often bilateral
subtypes.details: Endemic in temperate regions; may have preceding erythema migrans, flu-like symptoms; serologic testing required; treated with doxycycline

subtypes.name: Recurrent Bell Palsy
subtypes.description: Two or more episodes of acute peripheral facial palsy over time
subtypes.details: May be ipsilateral or contralateral; consider Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome (recurrent facial palsy, fissured tongue, orofacial edema) if associated features present; higher familial incidence

mimics: Ramsay Hunt syndrome (VZV): vesicular eruption in ear canal, severe otalgia, sensorineural hearing loss
Lyme disease: bilateral facial palsy, erythema migrans, endemic exposure history
Stroke (upper motor neuron facial weakness): forehead sparing, contralateral hemiparesis, acute onset
Ramsay Hunt syndrome sine herpete: VZV facial palsy without vesicles (requires VZV PCR for diagnosis)
Guillain-Barre syndrome: bilateral facial weakness, areflexia, ascending paralysis, CSF albuminocytologic dissociation
Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome: recurrent facial palsy, fissured tongue, orofacial edema
Sarcoidosis: bilateral facial palsy, uveitis, parotid enlargement, hilar lymphadenopathy
Leprosy (Hansen disease): endemic areas, anesthetic skin patches, thickened nerves
Cholesteatoma: chronic ear disease, hearing loss, otorrhea, destructive lesion on CT
Parotid gland tumors: gradual onset, parotid mass, sensory changes
Facial nerve schwannoma or hemangioma: gradual progressive weakness, intratemporal mass on imaging
Acute otitis media or malignant otitis externa: ear pain, purulent drainage, pseudomonas in diabetics
HIV seroconversion: acute facial palsy may be presenting feature; testing in at-risk populations
Pontine stroke or MS plaque: UMN facial weakness (forehead sparing) with other brainstem signs

bedside: Schirmer test: measure tear production using filter paper strips placed in lower conjunctival fornix
Stapedius reflex testing: evaluate hyperacusis through clinical testing with tuning fork
Taste testing: apply sweet/salty/sour solution to anterior tongue and compare sides
Whispered voice and Rinne/Weber tuning fork tests to evaluate hearing
Blink reflex timing: bedside observation of spontaneous and voluntary blink
laboratory: No routine laboratory tests required for classic Bell palsy presentation
Varicella-zoster virus PCR from vesicular lesions (or from auricular swab if Ramsay Hunt suspected even without vesicles)
Lyme serology (ELISA then Western blot): in endemic areas or if bilateral facial palsy
Random blood glucose or HbA1c: screen for diabetes mellitus
HIV testing: if risk factors present
Antibodies to HSV-1 and HSV-2: not routinely recommended; low diagnostic yield
CSF analysis: only if atypical features (headache, meningismus, other neurologic signs); may show lymphocytic pleocytosis in Ramsay Hunt or Lyme
Antinuclear antibody (ANA): if connective tissue disease suspected

imaging.modality: MRI brain with contrast and facial nerve protocol
imaging.indications: Atypical features: slow progression >3 weeks, recurrent palsy, other cranial nerve involvement, severe pain, no improvement at 4 months
imaging.findings: Facial nerve enhancement in intracanalicular, labyrinthine, geniculate, tympanic, or mastoid segments; may show enhancing geniculate ganglion in Ramsay Hunt; may identify facial nerve schwannoma or hemangioma; brain MRI to exclude pontine lesions

imaging.indications: Suspected cholesteatoma, trauma, mastoiditis, otitis media, or bony lesion
imaging.findings: May identify bony erosion from cholesteatoma, facial nerve canal dehiscence, transverse temporal bone fracture, or infectious process

imaging.timing: Not indicated acutely; consider after 10-14 days if no improvement for prognostic assessment
measures: Compound muscle action potential (CMAP) amplitude on affected vs unaffected side
Blink reflex: R1 and R2 components
imaging.prognostic: CMAP amplitude >30% of unaffected side at day 14 predicts good recovery; <10% predicts poor outcome and incomplete recovery

management.protocolReference: American Academy of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery Foundation Clinical Practice Guideline: Bell Palsy (Baugh et al., 2013, updated 2022)

corticosteroids.dose: 60 mg daily for 5 days, then taper over 5 days (60 mg x 5d, 40 mg x 1d, 20 mg x 1d, 10 mg x 1d)
corticosteroids.evidence: NNT 8 for satisfactory recovery at 3 months; most effective if started within 72 hours of onset
corticosteroids.mechanism: Reduces facial nerve inflammation, edema, and intraneural compression within the fallopian canal
corticosteroids.contraindications: Active infection, uncontrolled diabetes, severe hypertension, active peptic ulcer, recent surgery

antiviralTherapy.dose: 1000 mg three times daily for 7 days
antiviralTherapy.indications: Not routinely recommended for classic Bell palsy; use in Ramsay Hunt syndrome (VZV confirmed or suspected)
antiviralTherapy.alternativeAgent: Acyclovir 800 mg five times daily for 7-10 days (if valacyclovir not available)
antiviralTherapy.evidence: No benefit of adding antiviral to steroid alone for Bell palsy (RCT evidence); antiviral alone inferior to steroid alone
antiviralTherapy.note: In Ramsay Hunt syndrome, antivirals plus corticosteroids significantly improves outcome

eyeProtection: Artificial tears (preservative-free) every 1-2 hours while awake
Moisture chamber or eye patch at night (ensure complete lid closure to avoid abrasion)
Taping eyelid closed at night using medical tape (hypoallergenic)
Ophthalmology referral if incomplete eye closure, corneal exposure, or keratitis
Surgical options: tarsorrhaphy, eyelid weight implantation, or canthoplasty for persistent lagophthalmos
supportiveTherapy: Facial muscle exercises: gentle massage and stretching to maintain muscle tone (not in acute phase)
Heat packs to affected side for comfort
Analgesics (acetaminophen or NSAIDs) for retroauricular pain
Soft diet, chew on unaffected side
Straw usage may be difficult due to orbicularis oris weakness
Good oral hygiene to prevent food debris trapping in buccal sulcus

surgicalManagement.indications: Highly controversial; not routinely recommended; some advocate for patients with complete paralysis and >90% degeneration on ENoG within 2 weeks
surgicalManagement.timing: If considered, must be within 14 days of onset
surgicalManagement.evidence: Insufficient evidence to recommend routine surgical decompression for Bell palsy
surgicalManagement.risks: Hearing loss, injury to facial nerve, surgical complications

options: Hypoglossal-facial nerve anastomosis: for complete paralysis
Cross-face nerve graft: for selected cases
Smile restoration surgery: masseteric nerve transfer
Eyelid loading with gold or platinum weight
Brow lift, facelift, or other static procedures for symmetry

pathways: Within 72 hours of onset: start prednisone 60 mg daily x5 days with taper; assess for Ramsay Hunt (vesicles, severe pain): add valacyclovir
Eye protection: artificial tears q1-2h while awake, lubricating ointment at bedtime, taping if incomplete closure
No improvement at 2-4 weeks: consider EMG/NCS for prognosis, MRI with facial nerve protocol to exclude structural lesion
Persistent paralysis at 3-4 months: refer to otolaryngology and facial nerve specialist for surgical rehabilitation options
Refer for facial physical therapy if synkinesis or incomplete recovery develops

pathophysiology.primaryMechanism: Inflammatory edema and compression of the facial nerve within the narrow fallopian canal of the temporal bone
proposedTriggers: HSV-1 reactivation from geniculate ganglion: most widely accepted hypothesis (HSV-1 DNA found in facial nerve endoneurial fluid of some patients)
VZV reactivation (Ramsay Hunt syndrome): distinct entity with direct viral cytopathic effect
Inflammatory demyelination: immune-mediated nerve injury with predominant demyelination and variable axonal loss
Ischemia: vascular compromise of vasa nervorum from edema within the bony canal
anatomicalPredisposition: Facial nerve travels through the narrowest segment of the fallopian canal (labyrinthine segment, 0.68 mm diameter)
Nerve occupies 80-100% of canal cross-sectional area; any swelling causes compression and ischemia
This bony confinement predisposes to compression neuropathy
Edema leads to venous congestion, then arterial ischemia, creating a vicious cycle
histopathology: Early stage: edema, inflammatory cell infiltration (lymphocytes, macrophages)
Subacute: demyelination with axonal preservation in mild cases; axonal degeneration in severe cases
Chronic: fibrosis and loss of nerve fibers in cases with incomplete recovery
Aberrant regeneration: misdirected axonal regrowth leads to synkinesis (e.g., eye closure with mouth movement, crocodile tears)
virusSpecific: HSV-1: latent in geniculate ganglion, reactivation triggers inflammatory cascade
VZV: lytic infection in geniculate ganglion with extensive inflammation and hemorrhagic necrosis in Ramsay Hunt syndrome
Lyme: spirochete invasion of nerve tissue with inflammatory response

complications: Corneal ulceration and keratitis from exposure (lagophthalmos): most serious acute complication, can lead to vision loss
Synkinesis: aberrant regeneration causing unwanted facial movements (mouth moves when trying to close eye; eye closes when smiling)
Hemifacial spasm: aberrant regeneration causing involuntary muscle contractions
Crocodile tears (Bogorad syndrome): gustatory lacrimation from aberrant parasympathetic regeneration
Facial contracture: permanent muscle shortening and asymmetry
Chronic facial asymmetry and disfigurement leading to social and psychological distress
Persistent motor deficit with incomplete recovery (15% unsatisfactory recovery)
Psychological impact: depression, anxiety, social withdrawal, reduced quality of life
mustNotMiss: Promptly differentiate Bell palsy from Ramsay Hunt syndrome: look for vesicles in ear canal and auricle, assess for severe otalgia
Ramsay Hunt sine herpete: suspect when facial palsy is accompanied by severe ear pain or hearing loss without visible vesicles
Upper motor neuron facial weakness (stroke) spared the forehead - this is the critical distinguishing feature on examination
Bilateral facial palsy is NOT Bell palsy - consider Guillain-Barre, Lyme disease, sarcoidosis, and meningeal processes
Slowly progressive facial palsy over weeks to months suggests neoplasm (facial nerve schwannoma, parotid tumor)
If no improvement by 3-4 months, must do MRI to exclude facial nerve tumor or structural lesion
Any other cranial nerve involvement (hearing loss, trigeminal sensory loss, abducens palsy) indicates alternative diagnosis
emergencyPriority: LOW-MODERATE - Not life-threatening; urgent evaluation within 24-48 hours for best steroid benefit window
diagnosticClues: Acute onset (<72 hours) of unilateral lower motor neuron facial weakness including forehead
Isolated CN VII palsy with no other cranial nerve involvement
Retroauricular pain preceding or accompanying the palsy
Bell phenomenon: visible corneal reflection when attempting eye closure
Normal ear examination (no vesicles, no discharge, normal tympanic membrane)
Hyperacusis on affected side (stapedius muscle paralysis)
Taste disturbance on anterior 2/3 of tongue
Absence of other neurologic signs or symptoms
exclusionClues: Forehead sparing indicates upper motor neuron lesion (stroke or CNS lesion) rather than Bell palsy
Vesicular eruption in ear, auricle, or oral mucosa indicates Ramsay Hunt syndrome (VZV)
Bilateral facial palsy rules out Bell palsy and suggests Guillain-Barre, Lyme, sarcoidosis
Slow progression >3 weeks suggests neoplastic process (schwannoma, parotid tumor)
Other cranial nerve involvement indicates alternative pathology (brainstem lesion, meningitis, skull base process)
Fever, rash, arthritis suggests systemic illness or infection
Recent tick exposure with erythema migrans suggests Lyme disease
Hypotonia, areflexia, ascending paralysis suggests Guillain-Barre syndrome
